coredata.dc:title: Surface modification of transition metals (TM: Mn, Fe, Co) decorated Pt-doped carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQDs) nanostructure as nonenzymatic sensors for nitrotyrosine (a biomarker for Alzheimer): Perspective from density functional theory
coredata.dc:creator: Benjamin, Innocent
Ekpong, Bassey O.
Abdullah, Hewa Y.
Agwamba, Ernest C.
Anyambula, Isaac A.
Adedapo, S. Adeyinka
Louis, Hitler
coredata.dc:description: To develop a method for the detection of nitrotyrosine (NTS), transition metals: cobalt (Co), Iron (Fe), and Manganese (Mn) decorated Platinum-doped carbon quantum dot (Pt@CQDs) nanomaterial surfaces were computationally investigated. This study employed density functional theory (DFT) at the d3bj-B3LYP/def2SVP level of theory to gain in-depth knowledge of the adsorption, selectivity, reactivity, and trapping efficacy of nitrotyrosine (NTS) as a biomarker for Alzheimer disease. Results of the adsorption energy of the systems increased as −46.661 kcal/mol, −45.308 kcal/mol, −666.673 kcal/mol, and −12.507 kcal/mol which correspond to NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Pt@CQDs. NTS_Mn_Pt@QDs system demonstrated the chemisorption as it has the highest adsorption energy. HOMO-LUMO analysis shows that cobalt decorated surface had the least energy gap of 1.586 eV while interaction with nitrotyrosine a decrease in the energy gap was observed particularly NTS_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs exhibiting values of 1.850 eV and 1.665 eV respectively, whereas, Co_Pt@CQDs and Mn_Pt@CQDs systems showed drastic increase in the energy gap to 2.404 eV and 2.719 eV. This collective finding of this study is significant enough to present these systems as potential models for the detection and trapping of nitrotyrosine biomarkers for the diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To develop a method for the detection of nitrotyrosine (NTS), transition metals: cobalt (Co), Iron (Fe), and Manganese (Mn) decorated Platinum-doped carbon quantum dot (Pt@CQDs) nanomaterial surfaces were computationally investigated. This study employed density functional theory (DFT) at the d3bj-B3LYP/def2SVP level of theory to gain in-depth knowledge of the adsorption, selectivity, reactivity, and trapping efficacy of nitrotyrosine (NTS) as a biomarker for Alzheimer disease. Results of the adsorption energy of the systems increased as −46.661 kcal/mol, −45.308 kcal/mol, −666.673 kcal/mol, and −12.507 kcal/mol which correspond to NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Pt@CQDs. NTS_Mn_Pt@QDs system demonstrated the chemisorption as it has the highest adsorption energy. HOMO-LUMO analysis shows that cobalt decorated surface had the least energy gap of 1.586 eV while interaction with nitrotyrosine a decrease in the energy gap was observed particularly NTS_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs exhibiting values of 1.850 eV and 1.665 eV respectively, whereas, Co_Pt@CQDs and Mn_Pt@CQDs systems showed drastic increase in the energy gap to 2.404 eV and 2.719 eV. This collective finding of this study is significant enough to present these systems as potential models for the detection and trapping of nitrotyrosine biomarkers for the diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Several diseases that affect the brain and the nervous system are reported yearly at a rapid rate which is of great concern. Alzheimer's disease is one of the known disorders that occurs as a result of neurological disruption, which is the most frequent cause of progressive cognitive and functional impairment [1]. Significantly, it is accompanied by short-term memory, behavioral change, etc., and occurs in middle-aged adults above 65 years however, a rare form of Alzheimer's disease (AD) affects individuals of the ages 30s, and 40s referred to as early-onset Alzheimer's [2,3]. Although the main cause of the disease has not been understood, however, it has been linked with the disruption of proteins and accumulation of some proteins such as Beta-Amyloid plaque protein, tau proteins in the brain, inflammation in the brain, and oxidative stress [4,5]. Even with the advent and epidemiology of the disease condition, the procedures for diagnosis and subsequent treatment of the disease have been difficult as it has to deal with interruption of brain matters [6]. However, there have been remarkable developments in accurately diagnosing Alzheimer's, involving neurological examination, APOE gene determination, brain imaging (MRI, CT, PET), and cerebrospinal fluid or blood testing in conjunction with medical history [7]. However, these techniques are limited in the information for accurate diagnosis as biosensors [8,9] will accurately and most specifically identify and detect a biomarker associated with the disease condition of which nitrotyrosine (NTS) can serve a great purpose as a biomarker for the timely, accurate, and more specific diagnosis of the disease. Also, due to the reliable and time-minimizing nature of sensor operation, they have found applications in gas detection (H2S, and nitric oxide) [10,11], organic molecules (hydrazine) [12], and food safety analysis [13].

Nitrotyrosine is a biomolecule formed by the fusion of nitrite and tyrosine which is generated as a result of the damaged cytoskeleton causing conditions like oxidative stress and neurodegeneration which progress to Alzheimer's disease [14]. Therefore, the compound can serve as a biomarker for diagnosing the disease. Work of literature has identified nitrotyrosine as a biomarker for the prognosis of various neurological conditions including neurodegenerative disorders, cardiovascular diseases, and inflammatory conditions [15,16]. A theoretical study using the density function theory method has been employed for the detection of nitrotyrosine for Alzheimer's disease by Okon et al. [17] with a metal-doped silicon carbide nanotube. Similarly, a DFT study of tin disulfide nanotube design towards the diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease had been carried out by Murti et al. [18], which showed a progressive result explaining the sensitivity of tin disulfide (SnS2) sensor against AβOs in mice serum indicating the potential of application Alzheimer's disease. Furthermore, computational studies have also been carried out by Murti [19], whose investigation yielded great results in sensing DNA damage in Alzheimer's disease. In the work, ss-DNA was electronically linked to TiO2/FTO surface and was predicted to be the most reactive site which was in agreement with their experimental study. Doping of carbon quantum dot (CQDs) nanomaterial with other metals has been studied and presented relatively good findings for example, Devi, and colleagues studied nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot for the detection of a nitroaromatic contaminant in water [20], also Pourmadadi et al. [21], carried out an extensive review to the properties that make CQDs a good fit as biosensor and the properties are higher stability, low toxicity, easy synthesis, high quantum yield, and photoluminescence, presenting transition metal decorated doped-Pt carbon quantum dots as a potential biosensor for nitrotyrosine. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been studied to determine the detection of folic acid in biological systems by Zhang et al. (2020), the CQDs nanomaterial was able to show significant detection of folic acid as it was able to quench an intense blue fluorescence at 434 nm wavelength. The study attributed the efficient sensing of the organic molecule to their selection and sensitivity of quenching [22]. The investigation by Dastidar et al., (202) has also highlighted the application of DFT to study the Carbon quantum dots for the detection and estimating the concentration of Zn2+ in blood plasma. The study went on to express a 6% detection of zinc in the blood which provides a competent framework for easy and early detection of blood deficient in ions [23]. Carbon quantum dots have similarly been analyzed for the effective detection of cytosine and 5-methylcytosine by Thonghlueng et al. (2023) from urine samples. Significantly, the carbon quantum dots based on a fluorescent sensor were able to employ optical properties for effective sensing of cytosine and 5-methylcytosine with detection spectrum of 43.4 and 74.4 μM demonstrating a very effective method for their detection and subsequent prediction of the progress of cancers [24].

With respect to the importance and urgency towards diagnosis of Alzheimer disease condition, this research is designed to fully and vividly harness the usability of the density functional theory (DFT) method for studying the detection, adsorption, and behavior of nitrotyrosine with metal decorated Pt-doped carbon quantum dots nanomaterial. The structural orientation was analyzed for all optimized systems to understand their bond lengths before and after interaction with the biomarker (Nitrotyrosine). Also, quantum descriptor parameters (HOMO-LUMO, energy gap, global softness, global hardness, electronegativity, chemical potential, and electrophilicity index) were examined to understand the reactivity of the systems. To understand the inter and intramolecular interactions that exist in the systems, NBO analysis was carried out. Furthermore, density of states (DOS) was done. Visual studies employed the Quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM) and noncovalent interaction (NCI). To understand the behavior and the influence of NTS on the metal decorated (Co, Fe, and Mn) Pt@CQDs, thermodynamics analysis, Lowdin charge analysis, adsorption energy, and sensor mechanisms were examined.

In this study, the investigation was carried out employing the density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the d3bj-B3LYP/def2SVP level of theory. The method was aimed at assessing the performance efficiency of individually optimized electronic nanocomposites in the sensing of nitrotyrosine (NTS), which is associated with Alzheimer's disease serving. The optimization process for Pt@CQDs, decorated with Corbalt (Co), Iron (Fe), and Manganese (Mn) was precisely carried out employing the functionality of Gaussian 16 [25] and GaussView 6.0.16 [26]. The computational method was selected as it is significant in facilitating and exploring the interaction of the studied systems at the molecular level as well as gaining knowledge of electronic structures, reactivity, and bonding mechanisms [27]. The Density functional theory (DFT) approach was employed significantly to predict and give an account of the chemical nature of the studied system for the detection/sensing of NTS biomarker [28]. For effective detection of nitrotyrosine (NTS). The investigation for the electronic and behavioral properties of the studied complexes/nanocomposites was done with the analysis of frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) and natural bond orbital (NBO) analysis, as well as density of states (DOS) derived from multiwfn package and plotted with aid of origin software [29]. Furthermore, the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO-LUMO) iso-surfaces were visualized with the functionality of chemcraft software [30]. Additionally, Multiwfn package was exploited to analyze the Quantum Theory of Atoms-in-Molecules (QTAIM), offering insights into intermolecular interactions. Non-covalent interactions (NCI) were also strictly analyzed, giving a deeper understanding of the non-covalent bonding interaction in the studied nanocomposite. This was further visualized using the visual dynamic simulation (VMD) software package [31]. Moreover, adsorption energy studies, and in-depth exploration of sensor mechanisms with parameters such as conductivity, ELF, work function, dipole moment, and charge transfer. Additionally, the effects of solvation and deformation energies were meticulously evaluated as well as molecular dynamic simulations, revealing the materials' responses to external perturbations. The studied methods collectively enhance d the understanding effectiveness and potential design of a biosensor for the disease condition. Further investigation was carried out to understand the structural dynamics of the nanosurfaces and their interaction with the nitrotyrosine adsorbate employing the BIOVIA material studio| Dassault Systèmes software [32] at 298K temperature for 50Ps and 100Ps duration.

The structural orientation of the nanocluster was determined by the individual bond lengths that constitute the nanocluster. The geometry of the compounds under study assists in understanding the stability of the various compounds and upon interaction with nitrotyrosine which is determined by the type of change that occurs in the bond lengths of the participating atoms [33]. The results from this investigation were generated and are presented in Table S1 of the supporting information and Fig. 1 (a) and (b) for surface and interaction respectively. The optimized structure of the studied systems was analyzed before and integration with the biomarker, and the specific bonds that were examined are, Pt36 – C7, Pt36 – C29, Pt36 – C9, and the bond between the nanomaterial and biomarker was analyzed on Co63 – O61, Fe63 – O61, Mn37 – O62, and Pt36 – O56. The bond length for Pt36 – C7, Pt36 – C29, and Pt36 – C9 for NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs had 1.957 Å, 2.016 Å, and 0.977 Å, respectively, of which upon interaction with the bare surface increased to 1.983 Å, 1.975 Å, and 1.978 Å. The result obtained from NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs structure in Pt36 – C7, Pt36 – C29, and Pt36 – C9 bonds were 1.968 Å, 1.979 Å, and 1.968 Å respectively, whereas, upon interaction with the biomarker for Alzheimer disease, they were a subsequent decrease in the bond length between the participating atoms within the system to 1.958 Å, 1.967 Å, and 1.958 Å respectively. Furthermore, the compound NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs had a bond length of 1.429 Å, 1.409 Å, and 1.429 Å respectively while they were a drastic increase in the bond length after interaction of the surface with the biomarker 1.975 Å, 2.043 Å, and 2.085 Å respectively. Similarly, the undecorated system had bond lengths of 1.956 Å, 1.961 Å, and 1.956 Å for the bond labels of Pt36 – C7, Pt36 – C29, Pt36 – C9 respectively, and upon interaction increased to 1.961 Å, 1.982 Å, and 2.003 Å. The increase in bond length of the systems is possibly a result of electrostatic repulsion, Van der Waals forces, and conformational change that occurs between the surfaces and the biomarker [34]. Several literatures have highlighted that a constant or decrease in the bond length of the system after interaction explains the stability of the system [35], a familiar result of a decreasing bond length was observed in NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs showing that it’s the most stable system compared to others. The bond that existed between the surfaces and the biomarker (Co63 – O61, Fe63 – O61, Mn37 – O62, and Pt36 – O56) had bond lengths of 1.899 Å, 1.832 Å, 1.919 Å, and 2.476 Å respectively, and still, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs system showed the list bond length of 1.8322 ÅÅ atFe63 – O61 labels.

This concept is significant in understanding the interaction between a biological molecule (biomarker) and the sensing material (adsorbent) in designing an efficient and effective biosensor material for Alzheimer's disease [36]. The strength of binding is determined by the binding energy between the transition metals (Co, Fe, and Mn) and the bear nanomaterial (Pt@CQDs) significantly influences the performance of the general biosensor, where a stronger strength of interaction based on a higher binding energy connotes sensitivity. Also, the strength of interaction reduces interference with external substances and factors and as well, measures the stability of the interaction. Reported articles have it that, a lower binding energy connotes a stronger strength of interaction and higher stability whereas, a lesser binding energy indicates lower stability and strength of interaction within the system [37]. The results from this investigation are calculated and presented in Table 1 and from the results, Co_Pt@CQDs, Fe_Pt@CQDs, and Mn_Pt@CQDs were seen to exhibit the binding energy of −0.842 eV, −1.859 eV, and −27.599 eV respectively. From the results, it can be noted Mn_Pt@CQDs had the least energy depicting a higher strength of interaction in the studied system owing to the small binding energy value and also explaining that manganese formed a more stable interaction with the sensor material. The results also show that Co_Pt@CQDs and Fe_Pt@CQDs had higher binding energies signifying a lower strength of interaction and lesser stability of these metals towards Pt@CQDs which may consequently lead to weaker adsorption of the nitrotyrosine biomarker, and presenting Mn_Pt@CQDs as the most promising adsorbent for the adsorption of the NTS for Alzheimer's disease.

Molecular dynamic simulation explains the position and velocity of particles or compounds giving more insight into the behavior and properties of the material. This is essential in the design of nanocomposites for the detection of several biological molecules [38]. It is significant in this study for studying the metal encapsulated platinum doped carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQDs) for the detection and trapping of nitrotyrosine for Alzheimer's disease providing a predictive measure for designing an efficient and reliable sensor for the biomarker. Very importantly, molecular dynamic simulation provides valuable information about the stability of the studied surfaces with respect to their systems employing the functionality of the total energy of the system [39]. Literature has reported the stability of a studied complex to be linked with a lower total energy i.e., a lower total energy represents a higher stability predicting the whereas a higher total energy signifies less stability which would suggest repulsion force existing between the participation atom of the material [40–42]. Further investigations such as that of Mahamiya et al. (2021) show that molecular dynamic simulation can give insight into the stability of the nanomaterial as the temperature changes from the initial through the final analysis. However, in molecular dynamic simulation, the temperature alteration from the initiation phase of prediction through the final stage would eventually lead to structural changes such as bond length and the bond angle of participating atoms [43]. However, the total energy is the potential and kinetic energy contribution at a particular temperature concerning the time of exposure. This presents results from molecular dynamic simulation in Table S2 which was observed that, upon the doping of the Pt@CQDs with metals (Co, Fe, and Mn) they were a drastic change in the total energy, synonymous with interaction with the nitrotyrosine (NTs) biomarker. Pt@CQDs was observed to have an initial total energy of 97.278 kcal/mol increasing to a final energy of 97.525 kcal/mol exhibiting an average value of 97.536 kcal/mol and a standard deviation of 0.047 kcal/mol. There was subsequent variation in the total energies of the surfaces after being doped with the transition metal, where, Co_Pt@CQDs showed a higher initial total energy and increased from 243.947 kcal/mol to 244.156 kcal/mol with an average of 244.192 kcal/mol and standard deviation of 0.041 kcal/mol. On simulation of Fe_Pt@CQDs, it was observed that the initial total energy value increased from 198.322 kcal/mol to the final energy of 198.610 kcal/mol producing an average of 198.593 kcal/mol with a standard deviation of 0.045 kcal/mol. However, upon doping of the surface Mn_Pt@CQDs, the was a drastic lowering of the total energy which saw the surface having an initial total energy of 77.738 kcal/mol increasing to 77.980 kcal/mol and with the average and standard deviation of 77.983 ± 0.044 kcal/mol. Substantially, Mn_Pt@CQDs surface is notably the most stable compared to other surfaces as it presents the lowest possible total energy in the molecular dynamic calculations. Furthermore, the interaction of the various studied surface (adsorbent) with the nitrotyrosine (NTS) biomarker which is the adsorbate, showed that NTS_Pt@CQDs produced the initial total energy of 124.639 kcal/mol which increased to final energy of 125.405 kcal/mol with an average of 125.370 kcal/mol and 0.073 kcal/mol as standard deviation. Similarly, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs system gave rise to initial total energy of 470.296 kcal/mol increasing to the significant value of 470.843 kcal/mol with an average and standard deviation of 470.809 ± 0.066 kcal/mol whereas NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs had 858.402 kcal/mol as its initial total energy which subsequently increased to 859.077 kcal/mol giving rise to average and standard deviation of 858.990 ± 0.067 kcal/mol. Also, the system NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs after theoretically calculated, presents the initial total energy to be 329.382 kcal/mol which also increased to the final energy of 329.942 kcal/mol with an average of 329.903 kcal/mol and 0.067 kcal/mol as the standard deviation from the mean. As presented in Table 3
, there is a significant increase in the energy for the interacting systems from their respective surfaces. It is worth noting that upon interaction with the NTS biomarker, the NTS_Pt@CQDs system showed the least total energy arising from the simulation of the systems at the temperature of 280.000 K, and depicts the most stable system owing to the relatively low total energy exerted. On further analysis an increase in the duration to 100 Ps maintained the same initial values of the calculated parameters, it was observed the same trend in the energy flow as they progressed to the final results, however, the potential energies were seen to decrease for all surfaces and complexes, which is similar to the former calculated at 50 Ps duration. These results for both the surfaces and interactions are presented in Table S2, which explains a more dynamic stability of the studied systems on increased duration. Further investigation was carried out to understand the equilibrium and mobility conditions of the interaction (systems) employing the values of Mean square deviation (RMSD) values for the graphing over a particular period [44]. The results are plotted and presented in Fig. 2 a and b was observed that MSD for the systems over time fluctuated between the range of 0.00–16, moreover, the peak RMSD plot was seen at Pt@CQD before interaction and at NTS_Pt@CQD upon interaction with the nitrotyrosine (NTS) biomarker. The trend in mobility after interaction of the nanocomposites with the biomarker at 50 Ps and 100 Ps are NTS_Pt@CQD > NTS_Mn_Pt@CQD > NTS_Fe_Pt@CQD > NTS_Co_Pt@CQD.

In DFT studies, frontier molecular orbital (FMO) plays a crucial role in predicting and aiding the understanding of the reactivity of molecules most especially organic molecules [45,46]. The FMO theory focuses on the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) which is the highest energy orbital in the molecule filled with electrons and the Lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the reacting species (molecules) [47]. Investigations for the reactivity and stability of the chemical species under study were determined by harnessing the power of FMO which delf into the utilization of energy gap [48]. Literature has reported that the energy gap between the HOMO and the LUMO is significant in the determination and prediction of the reactivity of the studied compounds. Notably, a lower energy gap is associated with a higher reactivity of the studied species due to the smaller energy required to transfer electrons from HOMO (electron donor) to LUMO (electron acceptor) [49,50]. The results of this investigation are theoretically calculated and presented in Table 2, which clearly shows the energy gap of the surfaces both decorated and undecorated Fig. 3 shows the orbital density. From the results, it can be seen that the undecorated surface Pt@ CQDs had an energy gap of 2.530 eV and the decorated surfaces Co_Pt@CQDs, Fe_Pt@ CQDs, and Mn_Pt@CQDs had an energy gap of 1.586 eV, 1.948 eV, and 2.700 eV respectively. Notably, it can be observed that the Cobalt decorated surface had the list energy gap of 1.586 eV implying it to be the most reactive surface and prone to nucleophilic attack. Furthermore, upon interaction with nitrotyrosine biomarker for Alzheimer's disease, they were an observed decrease in the energy gap of NTS_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs to 1.850 eV and 1.665 eV respectively, whereas, Co_Pt@CQDs and Mn_Pt@CQDs systems had a drastic increase in the energy gap to 2.404 eV and 2.719 eV. It is significant to note that upon interaction of the various surfaces with nitrotyrosine, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs had the lowest energy gap compared to other systems explaining the highest reactivity and the least kinetic stability. Similarly, the result presents the system NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs to have the highest stability and lesser reactivity. However, in a comparative study amongst the complexes, it was observed that on interaction of the surfaces with the bio-sensor, they were an increase in the energy gap which signifies stability exception of Fe_Pt@CQDs which on interaction with the biosensor, there was a decrease in the energy gap from 1.94752 to 1.66452 (NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs) which indicates a high reactivity and lesser kinetic stability compared to the complexes under investigation. Furthermore, other quantum descriptor parameters were calculated and presented in Table 2 embodying chemical softness (σ), chemical hardness (η), electronegativity (χ), energy of the fermi level (EFL), and electrophilicity index (ω) to give more insight into the electronic structure, properties, and also the overall behavior of the surfaces and systems under investigation. The stability and reactivity of the systems were further explained by the chemical hardness [51]. Also, the global softness delves into giving more insight into charge transfer and chemical reactions which are a result of resistance in the alteration of electron distribution [52]. Electronegativity goes as far as explaining the bonding and reactivity of the material/surfaces and the possibility of the participating atoms gaining electrons [53]. Also, the electrophilicity index tries to measure the strength of the interaction that exists between the acceptor molecule and the possible surrounding electrons [54]. This descriptor presents and explains the possibility of the studied surfaces to detect and trap the nitrotyrosine biomarker as described in the stability and reactivity in the earlier paragraphs.

Natural bond orbital analysis is a technique in DFT study employed in analyzing the electronic structure of the studied molecule and also gaining insight into the nature of chemical bonds, distribution of electron density, and the inter and intra molecular orbital interactions [55,56]. It can further explain the stability and reactivity of the studied compound drawing more inferences from the second-order perturbation theory or stabilization energy which allows for analysis of change in energy and bond energies which could be due to electron delocalization [57,58]. The results obtained as presented in Table S3, show that the surfaces decrease in the second-order perturbation energy [E (2) in kcal/mol] amongst the studied complexes and followed a sequential order Fe_Pt@CQDs > Pt@CQDs > Co_Pt@CQDs > Mn_Pt@CQDs. The highest and lowest stabilization energies for Fe_Pt@CQDs were observed at LP*(1)C7 - LP*(2) C29 and LP* (1) C9 - LP*(2) C29 with values 2032.59 kcal/mol and 2032.52 kcal/mol, respectively. For Pt@CQDs, the highest and lowest stabilization energies were observed at LP*(2) C9 - LP*(1) C29 and LP*(1) C7 - LP*(1) C29 with values of 1858.32 kcal/mol and 1857.89 kcal/mol, respectively. For Co_Pt@CQDs, the highest and lowest stabilization energies were observed to be LP*(1) C7 - LP*(1) C29 and LP*(1) C9 - LP*(1) C29 with values 1216.33 kcal/mol and 1216.32 kcal/mol, respectively. Finally, for Mn_Pt@CQDs, the highest and lowest stabilization energies were observed to be LP*(1) C7 - LP*(1) C29 and LP*(1) C9 - LP*(1) C29 with values 1173.85 kcal/mol and 1172.50 kcal/mol, respectively.

It is worth mentioning that adsorption of NTS molecules on the metal decorated surface brought about a decrease in perturbation energies for Iron, cobalt, and manganese decorated surfaces indicating the stability of surfaces, though for the Platinum doped surface, an increase in the perturbation energy was observed suggesting increased in conductivity of the studied systems. As observed in Table 4 NTS_Pt@CQDs has maximum and minimum stabilization energies observed at LP*(6) Pt 36 - LP*(5) Pt 36 and LP*(1) C29 - δ* C7 – Pt36 with values 3368.53 kcal/mol and 2930.73 kcal/mol, respectively. For NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, the maximum and minimum stabilization energies were observed to be LP*(2)C7 - LP*(1) C29 and LP*(1) C9 - LP*(1) C29 with values 1822.76 kcal/mol and 1805.67 kcal/mol, respectively. For NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs. The maximum and minimum stabilization energies were observed to be LP*(1) C9 - LP*(1)C29 and LP*(1)C7 -LP*(1)C29 with values 1124.14 kcal/mol and 1079.21 kcal/mol, respectively. Finally, for NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs. The maximum and minimum stabilization energies were observed to be LP*(1) C7 - LP*(1) C29 and LP*(1)C9 - LP*(1) C29 with values 963.64 kcal/mol and 915.27 kcal/mol, respectively. From the studied complexes, a slight decrease was observed in the second-order perturbation energies for the interactions from the surfaces this signifies that a high perturbation energy shows a wider band gap which leads to stability. The donor and acceptor of NTS_Pt@CQDs were observed to have the highest stabilization and the Low perturbation energy shows shorter energy gap, hence, leads to greater reactivity.

In this study, DOS analysis elucidates harnessing the electrons frequency patterns of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for the studied compound/surfaces before and after interaction with the biomarker (NTS) [59]. DOS analysis is calculated and plotted to give a significant insight into the electronic properties of complexes under investigation. This analysis is seemingly applicable in depicting the magnitude of state/orbitals feasible for the occupancy of electrons as they spin/migrate from one energy level to another [60]. It is of great significance to understand that, the orientation of the different plots based on the orbital contribution indicates either less or no available states/orbitals for electron occupancy. The density of states plots consists of the total density of state (TDOS), partial density of state (PDOS), and overlap population density of state (OPDOS) which was plotted to monitor the contributions of the molecular orbitals [61,62]. The total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) are represented on the left of the DOS plot and are often referred to as the bonding molecular orbitals. OPDOS is the anti-bonding molecular orbitals and is represented on the right [63]. The energies in the molecular orbital are characterized based on the contribution of the bonded atom of the complex under investigation which is represented graphically for which, these studies have hydrogen, nitrogen, iron, carbon, cobalt, manganese, oxygen, and platinum. Significantly, it can be clearly seen from the DOS plot in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) that hydrogen with blue stroke had the highest orbital contribution which implies that it’s a major determinant of material properties, participates in strong chemical bonding, influence the electronic materials behavior, and also play a critical role in determining the materials magnetic behavior. These curves are obtained by the augmentation function of a multiwfn software and it was observed that PDOS forms a major impact due to the Hydrogen atom in the complex under investigation before and after the interaction of the surfaces to the biomarker. Notably, the dominance of hydrogen atoms amongst other participating atoms (platinum, oxygen, nitrogen, carbon, and transition metals) in all the systems, although in different fermi energy levels. Before interaction, Co_Pt@CQDs, Fe_Pt@CQDs, Mn_Pt@CQDs, and Pt@CQDs surfaces were observed at fermi levels of −0.21 eV, −0.22 eV, −0.23 eV, and −0.25 eV respectively, whereas, after interaction the systems NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Pt@CQDs were observed at fermi level of −0.21 eV, −0.22 eV, −0.22 eV, and −0.21 eV respectively.

The idea behind adsorption energy studies in biosensors is crucial for understanding and optimizing the performance of these devices/surfaces under investigation. Furthermore, conducting adsorption energy studies in the biosensing of biomarkers (NTS) is to investigate and quantify the binding interactions between the recognition element and the target molecule on the sensor's surface [64,65]. This research employed the DFT/gd3bj-B3LYP/def2SVP level of theory to gain insight into the energy capacity of metal (Co, Fe, and Mn) decorated doped Pt@CQDs interacting with nitrotyrosine biomarker for the possible diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease. A higher negative adsorption energy has been reported by literature to imply a chemisorption whereas, positive values are physisorption due to the fact the system is endothermic. the theoretical studies had the values deduced by getting the difference between the energy of the adsorbate from the sum of the adsorbent and the biomarker [66] using the mathematical equation in 1 and the results presented in Table 3.
(1)
E adsorption = E complex - (E surface + E NTS )

As shown in Table 5 the results of the interaction between metal-decorated Pt@CQDs and NTS, there is a variation in the adsorption energy exhibited by the various surfaces, which explains the influence of decorated metals on the interacting species. In order to know the potential of the surface sensing and trapping the biomarker (NTS), the systems decorated with metal, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs had the corresponding energies of −46.661 kcal/mol, −45.308 kcal/mol, and --666.673 kcal/mol respectively whereas, the undecorated system NTS_Pt@CQDs had the adsorption energy of −12.507 kcal/mol which was the lowest adsorption energy. The results generated show the existence of chemisorption between the adsorbate and adsorbent. However, the adsorption energy observed in the NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs system showed a relatively higher negative value compared to other decorated system NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs and undecorated system NTS_Pt@CQDs. These results have a vivid representation of a concrete biosensor for NTS biomarker for the diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease and present the NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs system as the best fit with the highest negative adsorption energy. The trapping potential of the various systems based on the energy values derived in the order NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Pt@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lowdin charge analysis and the effect of d-orbital occupancy
ce:section.ce:para:
Investigating the distribution of electrons within molecules while concentrating on the distribution of charges among atoms, lowdin charge analysis is a computational chemistry technique [67]. Understanding ideas about chemical bonding, reactivity, and other qualities requires this analysis. Exploring the d-orbital projection of Lowdin charges is crucial when working with transition metal atoms to learn more about how they behave during adsorption processes [68]. For a complete understanding of adsorption, it is insufficient to rely solely on the total Lowdin charges assigned to atoms [69]. To get important insights into the adsorption phenomena, a more thorough analysis of d-orbital projections is carried out. Table 4 lists the results of the Lowdin charge study together with details on the d-orbital population, degeneracy, and overall net charge of the system under investigation. It is clear from studying these systems that the NTS_Pt@CQDs complex, with a d-occupancy of 145.040 atomic units, has the largest d-occupancy. Due to higher occupancy in the
d
x y
,
d
y z
,
d
x z
,
d
x 2
−
y 2
, and
d
z 2
orbitals, the d-occupancy has increased. The total net charge on the transition metal inside the investigated complexes is represented by the population of each d-orbital, which represents the number of electrons filling that orbital. A larger charge transfer of 0.00000025 atomic units is also shown by the nts_Fe_Pt@CQDs complex, according to the evaluation of QNet(TM). However, predictions regarding how the modeled complexes will interact with the target analytes, particularly nitrotyrosine, can be made by understanding the distribution and transfer of charges within the material. It is possible to better comprehend the electronic rearrangements taking place within the examined systems by making use of these differences in d-orbital occupancy, which provide essential insights into the adsorption process [70]. According to the information gathered, it is possible to tailor the ZnA2@Mu complex to achieve the best sensitivity and selectivity for nitrotyrosine biosensor applications. This complex has a greater d-occupancy of 10.011 atomic units. Overall, the complexes under study show excellent potential for use in applications involving biosensor

Quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM) is a topological analytical tool for investigating the bonding and antibonding interaction that exists in a system obtained in free space, which was developed by Bader [71]. It is been employed to understand the nature and manner of bond formation between and within (Pt@CQDs with Co, Fe, and Mn) surfaces and the nitrotyrosine (NTS) biomarker employing the functionality of the various topological parameters [72]. The topological parameters such as ellipticity, density of all electrons ρ(r), lagrangian kinetic energy G(r), potential electron energy density V(r), Laplacian electron density V2(r), Hamiltonian kinetic energy K(r), total electron energy density H(r), and electron localization function (ELF) assists in gaining in-depth understanding of the density of electron and delocalization of electrons, and stability of the compounds, complex or systems under investigation [73]. Published literature has reported covalent interaction to be determined by the values obtained for Laplacian electron density V2(r) and total electron density H(r), where a negative value of both parameters strictly indicates the existence of a rigid covalent bond interaction whereas, a positive value generated for Laplacian electron density V2(r) and negative value deduced from total electron density H(r) simplify a non or loose covalent interaction [74]. On a similar ground, the stability of the studied systems is established by the exhibition of ellipticity value less than 1 (<1) whereas values greater than 1 (>1) strongly establish the instability of the system [75]. Furthermore, the density of electrons localized in the investigated compounds is understood by capitalizing on the values generated for the electrons localization function (ELF). Notably, a higher ELF value between 0.5 and 1 strongly suggests the localization of electrons in that position of the studied systems based on their various critical points (CPs) [76].

The Quantum theory of atom in orbital (QTAIM) in this study investigated the stability and bond interaction existing in the various systems after the adsorption of NTS by Pt@CQDs, Co_Pt@CQDs, Fe_Pt@CQDs, and Mn_Pt@CQDs. The results for this study had been extracted theoretically and presented in Table 5 which could be observed that Laplacian electron density V2(r) showed positive values in the range of 0.137–0.965 for the studied systems. However, the total electron density H(r) presented negative values for all critical points in NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, Positive values for C11-o55 and Pt36–O56 bonds, and negative results for Fe63-c8 in the NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, system. Further analysis showed that C34–H46, C29–H54, and C24–O63 have positive values whereas Pt36–N59 and Mn37–O62 had negative values in NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs. NTS_Pt@CQDs system was observed to generate positive values in all analyzed CPs as presented in Table 5. It is worthy of note that NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Pt@CQDs showed the presence of a noncovalent interaction due to the positive-negative values calculated values for the analyzed parameters, however, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs systems showed a partial covalent interaction which was in agreement with noncovalent nature of the systems in the NCI plot. The ellipticity parameter explains the stability of the systems. The results presented in Table 5 showed ellipticity values less than 1 (<1) as O55 – C2, H5 – C6, Co63 – O61, and Pt36 – O62 bonds of NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs systems had the values of −1.619, 0.394, 0.042, and 0.379 respectively. Also, the NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs system showed values of 0.324, 0.163, and 0.423 for C11-o55, Fe63-c8, and Pt36–O56 respectively. Alternatively, NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs system showed some bonds having ellipticity higher greater than 1 (>1) as C34–H46, C29–H54, C24–O63, Pt36–N59, and Mn37–O62 have 0.022, 1.019, 1.086, 0.313, and 0.218 respectively. In a similar vein NTS_Pt@CQDs in the bonds C11–H44, C9–H51, C3–O61, and Pt36–O56 had variable values of which somewhere higher than 1, the bonds recorded the values of 0.040, 1.508, 0.342, 0.299, and 0.625 respectively, presenting NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs and NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs as the most stable amongst other studied systems. Furthermore, it was observed that electron was evenly distributed and highly delocalized the various systems as the values generated are less than 1. although, some bonds express higher ELF value above 0.5 indicating possible electron localization around this bond.

In order to examine and visually represent non-covalent interactions that take place within molecules and chemical complexes, quantum chemistry frequently uses the computational technique known as NCI, or Non-Covalent Interaction analysis [77]. The weak forces that make up these non-covalent interactions include hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and stacking of ions [78]. By closely examining the electron density inside the system under study, NCI analysis aims to reveal key features of these interactions, including their spatial distribution, strength, and inherent properties. NCI produces what are referred to as NCI plots as part of this investigation [79]. These graphs use a color scheme, usually with blue designating attractive interactions, green designating regions with zero-flux or negligible interactions, and red designating repulsive interactions. NCI plots use these color codes to highlight specific molecular regions of interest, providing important insights into the behavior and stability of chemical systems [80]. In the current work, Fig. S1 illustrates the non-covalent interactions (NCI) and emphasizes one specific type of interaction. Interesting findings in many systems are revealed by the analysis. Firstly, the NTS_Pt@CQDs system's predominately green sections point to weak interactions, primarily van der Waals forces. The NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs systems, however, also exhibit small green fragments, indicating equally weak van der Waals interactions in these situations. Interestingly, a larger blue zone is seen between the nitrotyrosine molecule and the sensor material in the NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs system. In this particular system, the presence of blue indicates a hydrogen bond connection. This work efficiently analyzes the numerous kinds of interactions that occur inside metal-functionalized nanoclusters and identifies how each one contributes to stability. This provides a profound grasp of the intermolecular interactions at work and sheds light on the complex dynamics within these chemical systems.

Several parameters are considered in the determination of the sensor mechanism of compounds against the biomarker, these may be work function (Φ), recovery time (τ), charge transfer (Qt), conductivity (σ), dipole moment, etc. This assists in gaining a vivid understanding of the reactivity, adsorption efficacy, and efficiency of the chemical species against the biomarker as presented in Table 6 .

Work function is known as the lowest amount of energy that is required to dislodge the energy barrier that must be overcome to facilitate electron transfer between the nanomaterial (Pt@CQDs with Co, Fe, and Mn) and the biomarker which in this case is nitrotyrosine (NTS). Furthermore, the binding of a biomolecule to the sensing surfaces can be influenced by the work function which is often used as a signal in biosensing [81]. The determination of this work function is calculated using equation 2.
(2) Φ = -Ef l

Notably, the work function is derived from the negative value of the fermi energy level, therefore implies that the work function is directly affected by the fermi energy where an increase/decrease will tend to influence the value. In this research, the various systems NTS_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs had the work function −3.300 eV, −3.105 eV, −3.108 eV, and −4.193 eV respectively. It can be observed from the result that the NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs system has the least work function which is synonymous with energy needed to release electrons thereby breaking the energy barrier.

The separation of charges within the studied interacting nanomaterial can be understood by determining the efficiency of the dipole moment. Similarly, the information obtained from the dipole moment can go as far as explaining the strength of interaction that will exist between the biosensor and the nitrotyrosine (NTS) biomarker [82]. the results presented in Table 6 were theoretically calculated using the UB3LYP/def2SVP basic set for all the systems. Literature has reported the values of dipole moments to can be used to explain the sensitivity of the biosensor which would be significant in detecting biomolecules. A higher dipole moment will imply a higher sensitivity of the biosensor (Pt@CQDs with Co, Fe, and Mn) to the nitrotyrosine (NTS) biomarker [83]. the systems NTS_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs in this investigation presented the values of 46.395 eV, 45.144eV, 34.967 eV, and 30.776 respectively, and from these results, NTS_Pt@CQDs shows maximum sensitivity and high charges separation due to it higher dipole moments compared to other systems been investigated.

The role of conductivity is very critical in the design of biosensors due to its usage in the quantification of biomolecules (biomarkers). conductivity goes further to give more information on the concentration of the biomarker which is a function of getting the difference between the highest unoccupied molecular orbital and the lowest occupied molecular orbital generally referred to as band gap [84]. According to Gber and colleagues, a lower or decreasing band gap significantly implies a high conductivity in the nanomaterial [85]. Therefore, a subsequent alteration of the values of the highest unoccupied molecular orbitals and lowest occupied molecular orbital, will lead to influence on the electrical conductivity of the sensor material. The conductivity of the studied systems can be analyzed using equation (3)
.
(3) σ = AT3/2 e (−Eg/2kT)

where, A is the coefficient dependent on the nanomaterial, T is the temperature of sensing, Eg is the band gap of the material determined by the difference of HOMO and LUMO, k is the Boltzmann constant, e(−Eg/2kT) is the exponential decay of the conductivity.

There is always a transfer of charges between different molecules when they interact, therefore the charge transfer that exists in the surfaces (Pt@CQDs with Co, Fe, and Mn) to the nitrotyrosine (NTS) and the biomarker (NTS) where being examined with the aid of the charge that is present in Pt36 – O56, Co63 – O61, Fe63 – O61, and Mn37 – O62 for NTS_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs systems respectively. The result from the charge transfer analysis goes far to explain the operation and the ability of the biosensor to detect the specific nitrotyrosine (NTS) [86], hence the results from the analysis are presented in Table 6 presented from the nanomaterial, the systems NTS_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs had charge transfer values of 1.562 eV, 0.976 eV, 0.863 eV, and 1.293 eV respectively, explaining the higher value 1.562 eV attained for Pt@CQDs to imply a strong charge transfer between the surface and the biomarker. it is mathematically determined using equation (4)
.
(4)
Qt = Q ads. – Q iso.
Where Q ads is the charge on the adsorbate while, Q iso is the charge on the adsorbent.

Fermi level values derived are a clear indication of the influence of the biomarker on the reaction or interaction. However, this is significant in understanding the migration of electrons through different states and energy levels in the system [87]. It is often seen in the midpoint between the conduction band and the valance band orbital thereby representing the point of transitions of electrons. Significantly, a high fermi level depicts a tendency of the biosensor to be electrically conductive. For this research fermi level was calculated and presented in Table 6 with equation (5)
.
(5)
E FL
=
E HOMO
+
E LUMO
2

From the results derived in this investigation, NTS_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs, NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs, and NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs systems was observed to have the values 3.300 eV, 3.105 eV, 3.108 eV, and 4.193 eV respectively. The system that showed the highest conductivity was NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs, although other systems showed energy significant for high electrical conductivity.

This study focused on determining the efficiency of Platinum doped-Carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQDs) nanomaterial and the respective decorated metal surfaces Co_Pt@CQDs, Fe_Pt@CQDs, and Mn_ Pt@CQDs in the adsorption of ntrotyrosine serving as a biomarker for Alzheimer's disease, employing Density functional theory. The study was carried out using DFT/UB3LYP/def2SVP level of theory, harnessing the functionality of different theoretical approaches. It was observed that the systems during geometry optimization showed relatively small bond length and NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs showing to be more promising as it presented most bond length to be reducing after interaction of the surfaces with the adsorbate. Furthermore, molecular dynamic simulation presented NTS_Pt@CQDs to most stable systems as it showed the lowest total energy of 125.405 kcal/mol after the calculation although NTS_Mn_ Pt@CQDs also showed promising results with the total energy of 329.942 kcal/mol. Similarly, Frontier Molecular orbital (FMO) and quantum descriptor parameters explains the that NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs showed the largest energy gap of 2.719 eV explaining it stability and it possibly high performance. However, adsorption of the adsorbate on the doped surfaces showed results that explains a high possibility of the systems resenting NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs systems as the best post with the highest adsorption energy of −666.673 kcal/mol compared to other systems showing high physisorption potential of the studied nanocomposite for the detection of NTS biomarker, although the result projects in the order NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Co_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Fe_Pt@CQDs > NTS_Pt@CQDs. The sensor mechanisms go far to analyze several parameters with the notable work function with a consideration in their changes in the energy gap, and in a similar vein NTS_Mn_Pt@CQDs showed highest electrons transfer from the EFL analysis. Topology analysis was carried out with QTAIM and NCI for the non-covalent interaction concurrently agreed the existence of weak Vander Waal interaction for the various systems exception of NTS_Pt@CQDs system whereas all systems showed a noncovalent interaction. This theoretical analysis carried out are able to explain the efficiency of the studied nanocomposite in the detection of nitrotyrosine and producing a modeled biosensor for Alzheimer's disease.
All other information regards to this study are presented in the supporting information.
